Title: Biosynthesis of the allelopathic alkaloid gramine in barley by a cryptic oxidative rearrangement

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: barley; Nicotiana benthamiana; Arabidopsis; baker's yeast
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Hordeum vulgare; Nicotiana benthamiana; Arabidopsis thaliana; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Initial committed conversion from tryptophan to aminomethylindole
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes a cryptic oxidative rearrangement of L-tryptophan to an iminium intermediate that hydrolyzes to aminomethylindole (AMI) and glyoxylic acid
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome P450 monooxygenase, CYP76 family, CYP76M subfamily; heme-dependent enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against aphids, fungi, viruses, and other plants; negatively affects palatability to ruminants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables AMI production and, with NMT, gramine biosynthesis; knockout abolishes gramine accumulation in barley
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Pan-genome analysis showed AMIS presence strongly correlates with gramine-producing barley accessions. 2. Heterologous expression of AMIS produced AMI in Nicotiana benthamiana, Arabidopsis thaliana, and Saccharomyces cerevisiae. 3. Coexpression of AMIS with NMT enabled gramine production in heterologous hosts and in gramine-free barley cv. Golden Promise. 4. Cas9-mediated knockout of AMIS in barley cv. Tafeno eliminated detectable AMI and gramine. 5. Microsome assays demonstrated that AMIS is a cytochrome P450 requiring NADPH, oxygen, and redox partners and catalyzing an unusual oxidative rearrangement via an iminium intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step; essential committed-step enzyme for gramine formation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutively expressed transgenically in Arabidopsis and barley experiments; endogenous expression pattern not reported
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: d35S constitutive promoter in barley; constitutive expression in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: oxygen availability; NADPH dependence; presence of CPR and CYB5 redox partners affects activity
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increasing gramine may improve defense but reduce fodder palatability to ruminants
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A primary target for breeding or editing to tune gramine content in barley; can be introduced with NMT to engineer gramine production in other hosts
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A naturally occurring S211W variant caused loss of activity in cultivar Du-Li Huang; some nonproducing accessions carry transposon-associated intron insertions in the CYP allele, possibly linked to silencing
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Possible transposon-associated silencing inferred for alleles with retrotransposon insertion; no direct epigenetic assay reported
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pan-genome correlation analysis; transient expression; stable transformation; barley cotransformation; Cas9 knockout; yeast reconstitution; microsome enzyme assays; isotope-labeling; iminium trapping with NaBH4; metabolite profiling; NMR
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Barley pan-genome v1.0 analysis; no accession reported in provided text

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: barley; Nicotiana benthamiana; Arabidopsis; baker's yeast
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Hordeum vulgare; Nicotiana benthamiana; Arabidopsis thaliana; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal N-methylation steps from AMI to MAMI to gramine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product through sequential methylation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Sequentially methylates AMI to MAMI and then gramine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against aphids, fungi, viruses, and other plants; negatively affects palatability to ruminants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required together with AMIS to convert AMI into gramine
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NMT is the previously described downstream enzyme in gramine biosynthesis. 2. Coexpression with AMIS in Nicotiana benthamiana, Arabidopsis thaliana, Saccharomyces cerevisiae, and barley cv. Golden Promise enabled gramine production. 3. NMT alone did not produce AMI or gramine in heterologous plants, consistent with dependence on AMIS-derived substrate. 4. In yeast coexpression strains, MAMI accumulated as the monomethylated intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: required downstream enzyme; not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutively expressed transgenically in Arabidopsis and barley experiments; endogenous expression pattern not reported
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: d35S constitutive promoter in barley; constitutive expression in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Engineering NMT with AMIS may raise gramine and thereby reduce fodder palatability while enhancing defense
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Should be stacked with AMIS for engineering gramine production or modulating alkaloid levels in barley and heterologous hosts
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Located in a distant gene cluster with AMIS on barley chromosome 1H; presence correlates with gramine production across pan-genome accessions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient coexpression; stable transformation; barley cotransformation; yeast pathway reconstitution; metabolite profiling; NMR identification of gramine and MAMI
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Barley pan-genome v1.0 analysis; no accession reported in provided text
